tweets.text:
Paul Tudor Jones on what he learned from Warren Buffet:

 "I used to teach a class and my big lesson is you're going to make your money by riding a trend for the longest time.   

I used to sit there and rail on Warren Buffett year after year. 

I'd say he just happened to be in the right place at the right time and caught this bull market.  

Our fund has a minus 0.12 correlation with the S&P 500 over 40 years. So 100% of our returns are alpha. 

That's the difference between investing and trading.  

I was just thinking, why couldn't I be Warren Buffett? Just believe in America, and when you're down 50%, who cares, because America's gonna bring you through.

I feel like I've been a right guard in the NFL for 50 years, fighting in the trenches every day.  

I've always envied that belief system. It's worked so well for so long. 

He understood the power of compound interest at nine years old. What a genius."

The memory trade is not one big assumption; it’s simply linear math compounded across tokens, sessions, workers and concurrency

At 100K tokens per live agent, one trillion concurrent agents would need roughly 16 zettabytes of memory

To put that into context; that’s enough capacity to fill 60+ billion 256GB iPhones, but in the form of scarce HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) soldered beside GPUs

I’d agree with zeitgeist that it’s more likely than not, memory stocks and complimentary crypto AI equivalents will continue up and to the right 

Would an analysis of crypto AI memory projects be useful?

Japan committing multiple trillions to the AI buildout over the next decade. Other govs will do the same. 

We are going to see more robots than humans 10 years from now. The robot buildout hasn't even started. These companies aren't even public yet. There is still a huge energy need, the AI buildout isnt complete. It also is going to take years 

We are going to see rapid advancements in medicine as a result of AI. Speeding up time to market, research, and finding new cures. 

There are going to be many bubbles, and side bubbles inside the broader bubble and super cycle.

I have a thesis that the OG Web3 is dead.
Everything is moving to Web2.5.

@chainlink case is one of the many – look what happened ↓

Chainlink Labs joined Project Pangea, a coalition of 47 banks targeting real-time stablecoin-based FX settlement across the Europe–South Korea trade corridor.

The project aims to replace T+2 with near-instant T+0 atomic PvP settlement using regulated euro- and won-pegged stablecoins.

This is a meaningful institutional adoption signal for Chainlink’s oracle and cross-chain infrastructure.

Chainlink will become the boring infra for classic finance.

Is it good? Yeah.
Is it changing crypto as we know it?
Also yeah.

tweets.text:
Look at the comments under this and ask yourself why it only now feels so comfortable to be bearish; why it feels like much lower is guaranteed and therefore why even bother being an ‘early’ buyer at 60? 

Everything has been perfectly aligned for lower prices: 4y cycle + summer + Strategy fud + perceived quantum risks — and yet: since putting in the February low at 60, the market has failed to break meaningfully lower at every opportunity 

And each day that passes by *without* lower prices, the chorus grows louder that lower prices are coming and are inevitable 

Being contrarian for its own sake is nonsense — but when the R/R profile of the setup is this skewed (sentiment+narrative utterly convinced of an opportunity to buy much lower in 3-6 months but data already suggestive of historical extremes), it’s hard not to look at that and think of ‘being greedy when others are fearful’

Perhaps we do get much lower; or perhaps we don’t get another bull market in Bitcoin for years to come — but I am seeing a golden opportunity here that is being met with complete apathy and disinterest

Very interesting statement today: $MU CEO predicts a multi-decade memory demand cycle driven by humanoid robots.

"Humanoid robots, he says, will require roughly ten times more memory than today’s Level 2+ autonomous vehicles."

"And that demand wave is set to begin before the decade is out."

Something as well as was "Over time, we expect the value of on-device AI combined with pent-up unit replacement demand to drive memory demand growth"

Which is also another trend (Apple Intelligence is currently dog, but I'm sure we'll see innovations with localized/edge AI). 

Feels like all the industry leaders from $TSM Chairman, $TSLA Elon Musk, to $MU CEO see humanoids as the next major trend so physical AI is probably next. 

I wonder if the world is going to have enough memory. Or if we'll see enough breakthroughs to shrink memory usage.

Think so. But in the meantime, I call my strategy: Diversified Losses.

With $AXTI, $SOI, $AAOI, and many others.

Had a massive drawdown recently, 
CPO exposure was hit the hardest (Foci, Msscorp, etc) and adjacent names, feels bad.

Probably lesson personally, I had too much concentration in photonics vs. memory/other sectors without weighting/hedging properly.

With Soitec, there's been a few negative institutional reports that I'd disagree with. 

Think AXT was hit harder in specific just because of float expansion/dilution concerns. AAOI, probably just brought down with the theme.

I can't give advice on buying, so completely up to you to make for cost averaging or entering positions.

But I do think we're still early in the Supercycle with photonics, there's bound to be corrections/crashes along the way up.

If my personal thesis is correct though, many of these names will have a major inflection point in midway through 2027 scaling up to 2028.

Markets don't typically wait to price things in advance, but some ideas might be a tad early or in the buildout given it's H2 2026 now.

Which is why it's important to build your own conviction.

tweets.text:
on the state of "digital credit"-

there has been a lot of noise around what is going on in the preferreds market, good theories, bad explanations, almost all certainly with uncertain proofs. at times like this when the future looks inscrutable, its important to reduce all abstractions into the most knowable facts to be prepared for the future. here are three bullets that id like to share in that spirit:  

1) mNAV and BPS have been intellectually inconsistent since i called it out now over a year ago. this particular reckoning is at some level a reflection of the pent up mismatch between expectation and reality. the industry needs to do better and im optimistic that after mr. market's voting machine the healing process can finally begin

2) despite its colloquial characterization, preferred equities are definitionally not credit in the sense of its legal protection afforded to debtors, and is absolutely not a money market fund replacement. analogies can sometimes be useful to draw heuristics but you obfuscate the core differences at your own peril

3) nonetheless, digital credit has found remarkable product market fit. over 10bn in monthly trading volume across only a few tickers is heroic for a new product category in finance, and remains one of the most liquid securities in our capital markets today. in fact,  it is singlehandedly the largest preferreds segment that has ever existed in US history and the largest issuance was the largest IPO in 2025. i expect these instruments to be issued by many strategically important companies going forward to raise flexible funds for strategic assets/needs

always remember that cryptos core mission is to fix the unfair manipulation of time by technocrats, to fix the physics of money by reunderwriting the concept of duration beyond the risk free rate by introducing continuous spreads in the most dynamic mechanism possible with the most amount of decentralized liquidity the world has ever known. thats why perpetual futures is the biggest TAM the global capital markets has ever seen in modern history, as is prediction markets, as will be perpetual prefs.

while some may be in despair with the current price action, i believe in hindsight this will be remembered as a historic moment to upgrade the industry, its leaders, its products, and most importantly its customers. you will always be served well if you just remember that every bet in crypto is ultimately an expression of two ideologies: volatility is vitality, and liquidity is liberty.

now go for a stretch, and strive to always be learning
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
BPS and mNAV when taken together are intellectually inconsistent-

BPS is per Share, so does not incorporate leverage (but should)

And mNAV, despite the name (NET ASSET VALUE, which excludes liabilities) is using EV (which traditionally INCLUDES debt)

These details matter

One of the biggest issues with these reports is companies never share source material so you cannot verify the claims and overstate numbers based on flawed assumptions. 

Think a lot of my success can be credited to being transparent about my data when possible.

I generally only trust reports on illicit flows from top investigators at these entities:

Firms: CryptoForensic Investigators or ZeroShadow 

Analytics companies: Chainalysis or TRM

Everyone else has a lower quality threshold for output or is incapable.

Slow Mist & Elliptic can be ok just a bit less consistent. 

Good onchain research is still rare due to a lack of overall talent and understanding.

tweets.text:
a book everyone excited about the agentic economy should read - Accelerando by Charles Stross

the main character has a metacortex - a distributed cloud of software agents surrounding him in "netspace," borrowing CPU cycles from nearby processors and spawning new sub‑agents to research opportunities while he moves through the world

these agents continuously negotiate over compute, bandwidth, and informational “payoff,” treating economics as a game where abundance has replaced scarcity

markets and games blur together—scarcity is an old "primate superstition", and the real contest is how elegantly you can route compute toward interesting problems

i continue to believe reading sci fi is the highest ROI activity for anyone who wants to build for the future
